deals_cleared.merger_name: AUB – further interest in KJ Risk
deals_cleared.merger_description: Austbrokers Corporate Pty Ltd (trading as **AUC**), a directly and wholly-owned subsidiary of AUB Group Limited (**AUB**), proposes to acquire an additional 43% of the issued share capital in KJ Risk Group Pty Ltd (**KJ Risk**) by way of a purchase of shares from the existing majority shareholder, Niven Industries Pty Ltd, as trustee for the Niven Industries Trust (**Niven Industries**) (the **Acquisition**).

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Austbrokers Corporate Pty Ltd (trading as AUC), a directly and
wholly-owned subsidiary of AUB Group Limited (AUB), applied for a
notification waiver in respect of its proposed acquisition of 43% of
the issued share capital in KJ Risk Group Pty Ltd (KJ Risk), as
described in the transaction documents provided as part of the
application (the Acquisition).
As part of the Acquisition, AUB will transfer its existing 49% interest
in KJ Risk to AUC.
On completion, AUC will hold 92% of the issued share capital in KJ
Risk, with the balance retained by Niven Industries Pty Ltd, as trustee
for the Niven Industries Trust, which is the existing majority
shareholder.

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, AUC, is a wholly owned subsidiary of AUB. AUB is an
ASX200 global insurance broking group with a portfolio that includes
General and Specialist Retail Brokers, Authorised Representative and
Broking Networks, Managing General Agents and Underwriting
Agencies, Wholesale Brokers, InsurTech platforms, and specialist
Claims and Loss Adjustment services.
The target, KJ Risk, is an Australian risk management intermediary
business. KJ Risk’s primary activities relate to discretionary mutual
fund (DMF) services, including DMF broking and management
services. KJ Risk also supplies general insurance broking services,
including corporate and private client broking and workers
compensation consulting services.
KJ Risk operates as an integrated member of AUB, conducting its
business exclusively within the AUB network and under the AUB
brand, governance and operational framework.
AUB and KJ Risk overlap in the supply of general insurance broking
services in Australia.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
• Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular:
a. there is a small horizontal overlap between AUB (and its
connected entities) and KJ Risk in the supply of general
insurance broking services in Australia
b. there are alternative suppliers of general insurance broking
services to customers in Australia
c. except for AUB’s existing interest in KJ Risk, AUB (and its
connected entities) does not supply DMF broking or
management services in Australia.
In these circumstances, the ACCC does not consider it necessary to
reach a concluded view on the likelihood of the notification
thresholds being met.
Given that material competition concerns are unlikely to arise, the
ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

The Trustee for Fat Shaft Property Unit Trust

deals_cleared.merger_description: MH South Brisbane Pty Ltd and SCHF Pty Ltd (together, **Star Hotels Group**) propose to acquire 100% of the business assets and freehold of Shafston Hotel, a hospitality venue in East Brisbane.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
MH South Brisbane Pty Ltd and SCHF Pty Ltd (together, Star Hotels
Group) applied for a notification waiver in respect of its proposed
acquisition of 100% of the business assets and freehold of Shafston
Hotel, as described in the transaction documents provided as part of
the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, Star Hotels Group, is an independently owned and
operated pub and liquor retail group, primarily operating pubs and
hotels on the eastern seaboard of Queensland and in South
Australia. Star Hotels Group operates various liquor retailers in the
local area surrounding the target, as well as 3 hospitality venues:
• Hotel West End, which offers on-premises liquor, dining,
gaming, and a retail liquor store
• Hotel Kingsley, which offers on-premises liquor, dining, and
accommodation, and
• The Garage Fortitude Valley, which offers on-premises liquor,
dining, gaming, and a retail liquor store.
The target, Shafston Hotel, is a hospitality venue in East Brisbane
offering food, liquor, and gaming, as well as a drive-through retail
liquor store.
Following the Acquisition, Shafston Hotel will be integrated into Star
Hotels Group’s portfolio.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
certain publicly available information, and had regard to the factors
in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010
(Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. the parties’ combined shares of the supply of on-
premises liquor, dining, and gaming, as well as the retail
supply of packaged liquor, within the local area
surrounding Shafston Hotel are estimated to be low
b. there are alternative suppliers of retail liquor and on-
premises liquor, dining, and gaming in the local area
and the broader Brisbane area.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_cleared.merger_name: Direct Couriers – Phoenix Transport
deals_cleared.merger_description: Direct Couriers (Aust) Pty Ltd (**DC**), a wholly owned subsidiary of Direct Couriers Group Pty Ltd (together, **Direct Couriers**), proposes to acquire 100% of the shares in Phoenix Transport Holdings Pty Ltd, along with its 100% wholly owned subsidiary, Phoenix Distribution (NSW) Pty Ltd (together, **Phoenix Transport**).

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Direct Couriers (Aust) Pty Ltd, a wholly owned subsidiary of Direct
Couriers Group Pty Ltd (Direct Couriers), applied for a notification
waiver in respect of its acquisition of 100% of the shares in Phoenix
Transport Holdings Pty Ltd and its wholly owned subsidiary, Phoenix
Distribution (NSW) Pty Ltd (together, Phoenix Transport), as
described in the transaction documents provided as part of the
application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, Direct Couriers, is an Australian provider of business-
to-business courier, interstate delivery, and warehousing services. It
services logistics, freight forwarder, commercial, and retail
customers across Australia and New Zealand, and operates a
number of sub-brands including ‘Direct Cold’ and ‘Direct Road’. Direct
Couriers focuses on same day point-to-point services.
Phoenix Transport is also an Australian-headquartered provider of
business-to-business courier, interstate delivery, and warehousing
services. It operates in New South Wales and Queensland, with
coverage primarily limited to Brisbane and Sydney. Phoenix
Transport services a mix of logistics, retail, and commercial
customers, with a stronger focus on third-party warehousing and
distribution, large-scale heavy-freight dedicated hire work, and on-
transport for warehouse relocations and other similar project work.
Both parties supply courier and interstate delivery services in
Queensland and New South Wales.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application, and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. the parties’ combined share of supply of courier and
interstate delivery services in Queensland, New South Wales,
and nationally is estimated to be low and the increase in
share arising from the Acquisition would be small
• b. there are alternative suppliers of courier and interstate
delivery services in Queensland, New South Wales, and
nationally.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_cleared.merger_name: SOILCO - Go Grow
deals_cleared.merger_description: SOILCO Pty Ltd (**SOILCO**) proposes to acquire 100% of the shares in Garden Mediums Pty Ltd (trading as **Go Grow**) from Lumime Pty Ltd and trustee of the Honey & Nut Unit Trust (the **Acquisition**).

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
SOILCO Pty Ltd (SOILCO) applied for a notification waiver in respect
of its proposed acquisition of 100% of the share capital in Gardens
Mediums Pty Ltd (Go Grow), as described in the transaction
documents provided as part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, SOILCO, operates organic waste recycling facilities in
New South Wales, ACT and Victoria, including a facility at Tweed
Heads in northern NSW.
SOILCO’s facilities process food and garden organic (FOGO) waste
from local councils, blending it with forestry and agricultural
feedstocks (and other suppliers’ inputs) to manufacture compost
and soil improvement products. SOILCO supplies these products for
a range of applications including urban landscaping and projects and
agriculture and horticulture production systems (orchards,
viticulture, turf farms, nurseries, and greenlife).
SOILCOs majority shareholder, Australian Waste Management
Investments is part of the I Squared Capital group (I Squared). I
Squared is a global infrastructure investment firm headquartered in
the United States.
The target, Go Grow, manufactures and supplies potting mixes,
fertilisers and garden products from a single site in Ballina NSW.
Part of Go Grow’s business involves processing locally sourced
garden waste to produce compost and mulch which is supplied
directly to retail customers, as well as farms and nurseries. Go Grow
does not acquire or process FOGO waste.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and

providers of landscaping and specialty soil, compost and

Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.

effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on

events.title: Barro - BQC Quarries and Burdekin Concrete - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Barro Group Pty Ltd (Barro) via a subsidiary proposes to acquire, by
asset purchase, the quarry and concrete business and specified
assets (the Target Assets) operated collectively by Colevale Estates
Pty Ltd, M&T Populin Pty Ltd and Twin Rock Pty Ltd as the BQC
Quarries partnership, and BQC Registrations Pty Ltd, BQC Sands Pty
Ltd and Burdekin Concrete Pty Ltd (the Sellers) (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

Barro is a seller and distributor of pre-mixed concrete, quarry
products and various associated products across Australia. In
Northern Queensland, Barro owns and operates Townsville Concrete,
a ready-mix concrete (RMX) plant which is supplied with hard rock
aggregates by its quarry on the Black River and coarse sand from its
sand quarry on the Haughton River.
The Target Assets are the Sellers’ hard rock and sand quarrying
operations in Burdekin and Bowen in Northern Queensland, as well
as Burdekin Concrete which manufactures RMX in Burdekin.
The reasons for the ACCC’s determination are set out in section 2.

determination_statement_of_reasons.text: When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines and interim merger process guidelines.
For the reasons given below, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into effect.
Overlap between the parties
Both Barro and the Sellers (the Parties) operate RMX plants, hard rock quarries and coarse sand quarries in Northern Queensland.

In Northern Queensland, Barro owns and operates Townsville Concrete, a RMX plant located in Bohle (northern Townsville). Barro’s RMX plant is supplied with hard rock products by its quarry on the Black River (north of Townsville) and coarse sand products from its sand quarry on the Haughton River (south of Townsville, in the Burdekin region of Queensland).
The Target Assets include:

items: a RMX plant in Ayr, in the Burdekin region
two hard rock quarries at Airville, in the Burdekin region, and West Euri, in the Bowen region
two sand quarries in Ayr and Mount Kelly in the Burdekin region.

determination_statement_of_reasons.text: Competition assessment

The ACCC has considered the effects of the Acquisition by comparing the likely future state of competition if the Acquisition proceeds against the continuation of the current state of competition (being the likely future state of competition if the Acquisition does not proceed).
Relevant areas of competition
The ACCC has considered whether the Acquisition is likely to substantially lessen competition in the supply of RMX, hard rock products and coarse sand products.
The ACCC considers that while Barro and the Sellers both have a presence in Northern Queensland, the Parties operate in different geographic areas.
Competition effects in the supply of RMX
The ACCC considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of substantially lessening competition in relation to the supply of RMX.

Based on information supplied by the Parties and third-party submissions, the ACCC finds that the Target Assets’ RMX plant (Burdekin Concrete) and Barro’s RMX plant (Townsville Concrete) operate in different geographic areas and therefore do not compete for the same customers.
Competition effects in the supply of hard rock products
The ACCC considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of substantially lessening competition in relation to the supply of hard rock products.

The ACCC has taken into account the Parties’ own evidence on cartage costs, natural geographic boundaries, distance‑based pricing effects, and third-party submissions. Based on these materials, the ACCC finds that Barro’s and the Target Assets’ hard rock quarries operate in different geographic areas and therefore do not compete to supply the same customers.
Competition effects in coarse sand products

The ACCC considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of substantially lessening competition in relation to the supply of coarse sand products. The ACCC finds that there is limited horizontal overlap between Barro and BQC, and the Parties are not close competitors.

The ACCC’s view, based on information and documents provided by the Parties including evidence on cartage costs, natural geographic boundaries, distance‑based pricing effects, as well as third party submissions, is that:

items: Delivery distance is a key constraint on substitutability of coarse sand suppliers as transport costs form a significant proportion of the delivered price of coarse sand.

Due to the distance between Barro’s Haughton River quarry and the Target Assets’ Home Hill and Mount Kelly quarries, Barro and the Sellers do not compete closely to supply coarse sand products to the same customers.

As the vast majority of coarse sand produced by its Haughton River quarry is used by Barro’s own concrete manufacturing operations, Barro and the Sellers do not compete closely to supply coarse sand products to the same customers.

Although the ACCC received mixed views from some third parties regarding the alternative sand quarries in the Burdekin region, the ACCC found that there are competitors that hold sand leases located in closer proximity to the Target Assets’ Home Hill and Mount Kelly quarries.
Barro does not have the ability to foreclose rival RMX suppliers’ access to coarse sand products as rival RMX suppliers also hold sand leases in the Burdekin region.

deals_cleared.merger_description: Quanta Services Australia Pty Ltd (**Quanta**) proposes to acquire 100% of the shares in PSD Global Holdings Pty Ltd, including ownership of its wholly owned subsidiaries, PSD Energy Pty Ltd and PSD Plant & Equipment Pty Ltd, and all assets constituting the business trading as PSD Energy (together, **PSD**).

determination_table_content.details:
Quanta Services Australia Pty Ltd (Quanta)’s acquisition of 100% of
the shares in PSD Global Holdings Pty Ltd, including ownership of its
wholly owned subsidiaries, PSD Energy Pty Ltd and PSD Plant &
Equipment Pty Ltd, and all assets constituting the business trading
as PSD Energy (together, PSD) (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Quanta, through its subsidiaries, is involved in
engineering, procurement and construction of infrastructure projects
in the energy sector, including high voltage (HV) electricity projects.
Quanta’s operations in HV electricity projects include transmission
lines, substations, grid-scale Battery Energy Storage Systems
(BESS), and the supply of electrical protection panels and
switchrooms.
The target, PSD, is an electrical engineering, design and construction
company specialising in HV electrical infrastructure and secondary
systems. PSD’s projects include substations, solar farms, BESS and
the supply of electrical protection panels, switchrooms and related
facilities.

Quanta and PSD both supply:
▪ HV electrical infrastructure design and construction services
(HV D&C services), including BESS design and construction
services
▪ electrical protection panels and switchrooms.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form, information from third partes and publicly available
information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings:
▪ The Parties do not appear to be close competitors in the supply
of HV D&C services or in the supply of electrical protection
panels and switchrooms.
▪ The merged firm would likely continue to face competition from
alternative suppliers of HV D&C services including UGL, Downer,
RJE Global and Monford Group, as well as alternative suppliers
of electrical protection panels and switchrooms including LAI
Industries, Mayfield Industries and Ampcontrol.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

deals_cleared.merger_name: Metro Petroleum – Retail sites part of divestiture remedy from Ampol-EG acquisition
deals_cleared.merger_description: Dib Group Pty Limited (**Dib Group**) proposes to acquire 41 retail fuel sites from Ampol Retail Holding Pty Ltd , a wholly owned subsidiary of Ampol Limited (**Ampol**), as part of Ampol’s proposed remedy offered to the ACCC in relation to its acquisition of all of the issued shares in each of EG Group Australia Pty Ltd and EG AsiaPac Holdings Pty Ltd (**Ampol EG Acquisition**).

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by a division of the Commission constituted by a direction issued pursuant to section 19 of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Dib Group Pty Limited (Dib Group) applied for a notification waiver in
respect of its proposed acquisition of 41 retail fuel sites from Ampol
Retail Holding Pty Ltd, a wholly owned subsidiary of Ampol Limited
(Ampol), as described in the transaction documents provided as part
of the application (the Acquisition).
The Acquisition arises from Ampol’s remedy offer to the ACCC in
respect of Ampol’s proposed acquisition of 100% of the share capital
in EG Group Australia Pty LTD (Ampol EG Acquisition), which
includes the divestiture of 41 retail fuel sites to an ACCC-approved
purchaser.

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, Dib Group, is an independent fuel retailer that operates
a network of over 300 retail fuel and convenience sites across New
South Wales, Victoria, Queensland and Western Australia, under the
Metro Petroleum (Metro) brand. Dib Group is also involved in
wholesale petroleum supply, fuel logistics and distribution, and
related petroleum industry activities.
The vendor, Ampol, is a vertically integrated fuel company with
upstream fuel production and importing assets, wholesale supply
and distribution facilities, and a network of retail fuel and
convenience sites across Australia.
Ampol owns the Lytton refinery in Brisbane, Queensland, where it
refines imported crude oil, and a number of terminals nationwide
through which it imports finished petrol products from overseas
refineries and trading houses.
Ampol operates retail sites under both the Ampol brand and the U-
GO brand. Ampol branded sites are full-service sites with fuel and
convenience offerings. Launched in 2023, the U-GO branded sites are
low cost, unstaffed self-service sites selling only fuel and with no
convenience offering. Ampol owns and operates 576 sites under the
Ampol brand, and 46 under the U-GO brand.
Metro and Ampol overlap in the retail and wholesale supply of fuel,
and the retail supply of convenience products in Australia. Of the 41
sites being acquired in 39 local markets, there are 4 local markets
where a Metro owned and operated site overlaps with one of the
target sites (Belrose, Raymond Terrace, Jerrabomberra, and
Childers).

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application
(which includes a copy of the Proposed Purchaser Notice provided
by Ampol in connection with its 22 April 2026 remedy offer in
respect of the Ampol-EG Acquisition) and other relevant information
available to it. The ACCC has also had regard to the factors in
section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010
(Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, and noting the ACCC’s
separate analysis of overlaps between Dib Group and the target sites
as part of its consideration of that Proposed Purchaser Notice for
the ACCC’s assessment of the Ampol EG Acquisition, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition.
In particular, in 35 local markets, the Acquisition would result in new
entry. In respect of the remaining 4 local markets where there is
overlap, Dib Group would be competitively constrained by a number
of alternate fuel retailers and convenience retailers.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

suppression and rescue technology including products offered under

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form and information from third parties.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC

• There is no horizontal overlap or vertical relationship between

deals_cleared.merger_description: Novartis Finance Corporation (Novartis Finance), via its wholly-owned subsidiary Elba Merger Sub, Inc., proposes to acquire 100% of the issued and outstanding capital stock of Excellergy, Inc. (Excellergy) (the Acquisition).

events.title: Novartis - Excellergy - Phase 1 Determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Novartis Finance Corporation (Novartis Finance), via its wholly
owned subsidiary Elba Merger Sub, Inc., proposes to acquire 100% of
the issued and outstanding capital stock of Excellergy, Inc.
(Excellergy) (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

Novartis Finance, the acquirer, is a subsidiary of Novartis AG
(collectively, Novartis). Novartis is a global medicines company
headquartered in Basel, Switzerland. Novartis is engaged in the
research, development, manufacturing, distribution,
commercialisation and sale of medicines, with a focus on the
following therapeutic areas: cardiovascular, renal and metabolic;
immunology; neuroscience; oncology; and established brands.
In Australia, Novartis supplies a range of medicines to customers,
including treatments for chronic spontaneous urticaria (CSU) such
as Xolair, and has a further CSU medicine under evaluation by the
Therapeutic Goods Administration.
Excellergy, the target, is a private biotechnology company based in
Palo Alto, California, United States. Excellergy is developing next-
generation anti-immunoglobulin E (anti-IgE) therapies for the
treatment of IgE-driven allergic diseases. This includes Excellergy's
lead asset, Exl-111, a half-life extended, high-affinity anti-IgE
antibody, which is currently in Phase 1 clinical evaluation.
Excellergy's operations are limited to the United States, and it has
not generated any revenue from sales of product in Australia (or
anywhere else) to date. Excellergy's connection with Australia arises
from the registration of trademarks and the establishment of an
Australian subsidiary for the purpose of conducting clinical trials.

determination_table_content.item:
Overlap and relationship
between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
Novartis and Excellergy (the Parties) overlap in the research and
development of therapies for CSU and IgE-mediated food allergy
(FA).

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form, information from third partes and publicly available
information, as relevant.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• The potential competition between the Parties in the supply of
therapies for CSU and FA is uncertain, given the early stages of
the Parties’ respective pipeline products, which are also likely to
be differentiated.
• There are other competitors who already supply, or are active in,
the development of CSU and FA therapies that will continue to
compete with Novartis.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

events.title: Seismic - Highspot - Phase 1 Determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Seismic Software, Inc (Seismic) proposes to acquire 100% of the
share capital of Highspot, Inc. (Highspot) by way of merger (the
Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

Both the acquirer, Seismic, and the target, Highspot, are US-
headquartered businesses which offer sales enablement software
platforms and products to Australian customers as well as globally.
Sales enablement software helps improve enterprise sales teams’
performance by providing content management and collaboration
capabilities, data analytics capabilities and learning and coaching
modules to build sales skills. Both parties’ sales enablement
platforms are enhanced by artificial intelligence. Both parties supply
their products to customers across a range of industry sectors.
Seismic is majority owned by Permira Advisers LLP (Permira).
Permira would remain the controlling shareholder of Seismic
following the Acquisition.

The parties overlap in the supply of sales enablement software
globally. The parties supply integrated sales enablement platforms
that offer a full range of sales enablement applications and also
offer each sales enablement application separately.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form, information from third parties and publicly
available information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market in
Australia. In reaching its decision, and based on the material before
it, the ACCC makes the following findings.
• The parties both have a limited presence in Australia, with few
customers and relatively low revenue.
• Most Australian customers of sales enablement software
acquire one or more single applications from the overall range of
sales enablement applications, rather than a full platform
solution. Information before the ACCC indicates that there are
multiple alternative suppliers of the range of single applications
offered by the parties. While some Australian customers use a
full platform solution for which there are fewer alternatives, they
did not express concerns about the impact of the transaction on
competition.
• The ACCC closely considered information, including third party
feedback, regarding Seismic’s strong position in the supply of
sales enablement software platforms and the potential for the
Acquisition to further strengthen that position. However, given
that alternatives, such as Showpad, will remain available, and
taking into account other information including third party
feedback, the ACCC found that customers would continue to
have a range of competitive options for sales enablement
software post-acquisition.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

Ochre Health Pty Limited applied for a notification waiver in respect
of:
• its proposed acquisition of all of the shares in Airlie
Women’s Clinic Pty Ltd (AWC), and
• Ochre Group Commercial Property Pty Ltd as trustee for
Ochre Group Commercial Property Unit Trust’s proposed
acquisition of the land on which 2 of AWC’s 3 general
practice medical clinics are operated,
as described in the transaction documents provided as part of the
application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirers, Ochre Health Pty Limited and Ochre Group
Commercial Property Pty Ltd as trustee for Ochre Group Commercial
Property Unit Trust (together with their connected entities, Ochre
Health), support independent doctors to provide general practice
medical services in regional, rural and urban communities around
Australia.
Ochre Health currently operates 69 medical centres and urgent care
clinics located in the ACT, NSW, Queensland, Tasmania and Victoria,
but currently does not operate any medical centres or urgent care
clinics in Melbourne, Victoria. Ochre Health Group’s medical
recruitment business, Ochre Recruitment, also works with hospitals
and medical practices across Australia and NZ to source and place
locum and permanent doctors across a range of specialities.
Ochre Health is majority owned and controlled by Genesis Capital, an
Australian-based private equity firm that invests in businesses
operating in the healthcare sector.
AWC is a family medical practice with an all-female team of doctors
providing comprehensive general practice medical care to men,
women and children from its 3 clinics in the southeastern Melbourne
suburbs of Prahran and Malvern, Victoria.
Ochre Health and AWC supply general practice medical services in
Victoria, however Ochre Health does not operate any medical
centres or urgent care clinics within 80km of the AWC clinics.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. there is no overlap in the supply of general practice medical
services in the local Prahran and Malvern areas or in the
greater Melbourne area
b. there are other clinics supplying general practice medical
services near Prahran and Malvern, Victoria and in the
greater Melbourne area.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.

notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on

deals_cleared.merger_name: INPEX – minority interests in Beetaloo development projects

targets.name: Daly Waters FSDA North Pty Ltd
Daly Waters FSDA South Pty Ltd

deals_cleared.merger_description: INPEX Corporation, through its wholly owned Australian subsidiaries - INPEX FSDA N&S Pty Ltd and INPEX BCD Pty Ltd (collectively, **INPEX**) entered into a farm-in agreement with Daly Waters Energy LP (**DWE**) to acquire:

events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
INPEX Corporation (INPEX), via wholly owned subsidiaries INPEX
FSDA N&S Pty Ltd and INPEX BCD Pty Ltd, applied for a notification
waiver in respect of its proposed acquisition of:
• a 20% interest in the Beetaloo Central Development project
• an 11.25% interest in the First Strategic Development Area
South project, and
• an 11.25% interest in the FSDA North project
(together, the Projects)
via a farm-in agreement with Daly Waters Energy LP (DWE), as
described in the transaction documents provided as part of the
application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, INPEX, is an oil and gas exploration and production
company headquartered in Tokyo, Japan. Its main business includes
research, exploration, development, production and sales of oil,
natural gas and other mineral resources, as well as investment and
lending to the companies engaged in these activities.
In Australia, INPEX:
• owns a majority stake and operates the Icthys LNG project in
offshore Western Australia
• has a minority participating interest in Prelude FLNG Project
in offshore Western Australia
• has a minority participating interest in Darwin LNG Project,
located in the Northern Territory
• has a minority participating interest in the Ravensworth Oil
Field in offshore Western Australia
• has a 50% interest in Potentia Energy.
Furthermore, INPEX is engaged in oil and gas exploration through
interests in leases in and around Western Australia and the Northern
Territory, principally in the area surrounding the Ichthys LNG Project.
INPEX also supplies LNG in the global seaborn LNG industry and
natural gas in the Northern Territory.
The Projects, which are the target of the Acquisition, are located
within the Beetaloo Sub-basin, an undeveloped but prospective shale
gas region in the Northern Territory.
The Projects would be operated as an unincorporated joint venture
between DWE (via its subsidiaries), Tamboran Resources
Corporation (via its subsidiaries) and INPEX (acting via its
subsidiaries) (together, the Joint Venture Parties), and would be
governed by separate joint operating agreements. The acquirer,
INPEX, would acquire a minority interest in each of the Projects and
would not be a project operator for any of the Projects.
Pursuant to the terms and conditions of the Retention Licences that
will be obtained for each Project and a joint operating agreement, the
Joint Venture Parties will explore for, and undertake appraisal
activities to confirm a commercial resource of conventional and
unconventional oil and gas resources within each Project area.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular
a. the Acquisition would lead to a very small increase in
INPEX’s share of gas or LNG production in Australia (to the
extent gas or LNG is produced from the Projects)
b. there are alternative producers and suppliers of natural gas
or LNG in Australia.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.

object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.

deals_cleared.merger_name: Volt Managed Investment Trusts – HMC Energy Transition Platform
deals_cleared.merger_description: Volt Managed Investment Trust 1 and Volt Managed Investment Trust 2 (together, the **Volt MITs**), through their respective trustees, propose to invest A$355 million in HMC Capital Limited’s Energy Transition Platform (**HMC Platform**) through the acquisition of loan notes and share warrants issued by Volt HoldCo Pty Ltd (**Volt HoldCo**) (the **Acquisition**), with the warrants automatically converting into a minority equity interest in Volt HoldCo upon repayment of the loan notes. HMC Platform comprises wholly-owned subsidiaries of Volt HoldCo.

events.title: Volt Managed Investment Trusts – HMC Energy Transition Platform - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Volt Managed Investment Trust 1 and Volt Managed Investment
Trust 2 (together, the Volt MITs), through their respective trustees,
propose to invest $355 million (AUD) in HMC Capital Limited’s
Energy Transition Platform (HMC Platform) through the acquisition
of loan notes and share warrants issued by Volt HoldCo Pty Ltd (Volt
HoldCo) (the Acquisition), with the warrants automatically
converting into a minority equity interest in Volt HoldCo upon
repayment of the loan notes.

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirers, the Volt MITs, are administered by professional trust
companies, namely Perpetual Corporate Trust Limited, and The
Trust Company (Australia) Limited, respectively (together, the
Perpetual Trustees). The single unitholders in the Volt MITs are KKR
Global Climate Transition Holdings Pte. Ltd and KKR Asia Climate
Holdings Pte. Ltd respectively (the KKR Funds).
The Volt MITs are ultimately wholly owned by investment funds,
vehicles and/or accounts advised and managed by various indirect
subsidiaries of KKR & Co. Inc. (together with its direct and indirect
subsidiaries, KKR).
KKR is a global investment firm that offers alternative asset
management as well as capital markets and insurance solutions.
The target, HMC Platform, operates energy generation and storage
assets and has a development pipeline of wind and energy storage
projects in Victoria. HMC Platform comprises wholly owned
subsidiaries of Volt HoldCo Pty Ltd, which is itself a subsidiary of
HMC Capital Limited (HMC). HMC is an ASX-listed alternative asset
manager.

determination_table_content.item:
Overlap and relationship
between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
KKR and the HMC Platform (together, the Parties) both operate
within the electricity generation supply chain.
The Parties horizontally overlap in the operation of electricity
generating and storage assets in National Electricity Market (NEM).
Some of HMC Platform’s assets and pipeline projects are, or will be,
connected to transmission infrastructure in which KKR has an
indirect and minority interest.
In addition, KKR, via a managed investment fund, has an indirect
minority interest in Transgrid and Victoria Power Network. One of
the HMC Platform’s Victorian assets is connected to a Victoria
Power Network distribution network, and three of HMC Platform’s
pipeline projects in NSW will be connected to Transgrid’s
transmission network upon completion.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form, market share data from prior matters and publicly
available information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• The Parties’ post-Acquisition share in the generation and storage
of electricity in the NEM is minimal, and the merged entity would
continue to be constrained by alternative operators of electricity
generation and storage assets in the NEM.
• KKR would be unlikely to have an ability to favour the HMC
Platform’s assets post-Acquisition given its indirect minority
interest would not give it control or influence over the relevant
network businesses.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

deals_cleared.merger_name: Ampol – Freehold land acquisition in Chelsea Heights, Victoria
deals_cleared.merger_description: The Principal Party is Ampol Petroleum Pty Limited (**Ampol Australia**). Ampol Australia is a wholly owned subsidiary of Ampol Limited (**Ampol**), which is listed on the Australian Stock Exchange (ASX: ALD). Ampol is a transport energy provider which engages in purchasing, refining, and distributing petroleum products in Australia and is rolling out electric vehicles public charging networks. In Australia, Ampol has a network of fuel and retail convenience sites, which it conducts largely through Ampol Australia.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Ampol Petroleum Pty Ltd (Ampol Australia) applied for a notification
waiver in respect of its proposed acquisition of a freehold interest in
land at 241 Wells Road, Chelsea Heights, Victoria 3196 (the Target
Site) via a contract for the sale of land, as described in the
transaction documents provided as part of the application (the
Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, Ampol Australia, is a wholly owned subsidiary of Ampol
Limited (Ampol). Ampol, which is listed on the ASX, is a vertically
integrated fuel company with upstream fuel production and
importing assets, wholesale supply and distribution facilities, and a
network of retail fuel and convenience sites across Australia.
Ampol operates retail sites under both the Ampol brand and the U-
GO brand. Ampol branded sites are full-service sites with fuel and
convenience offerings. Launched in 2023, the U-GO branded sites are
low cost, unstaffed self-service sites selling only fuel and with no
convenience offering.
The target is a freehold interest in land at the Target Site. Ampol
currently operates a retail fuel and convenience site under the Ampol
brand located at the Target Site. Ampol sets the fuel pricing at the
Target Site.
The vendor is a private property investor not involved in the supply of
any goods and services other than the current leasehold to Ampol.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers
that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening
of competition. In particular:
a. Ampol currently operates a retail fuel and convenience site
at the Target Site, has been leasing the land for 33 years for
that purpose, and intends to continue operating a retail fuel
and convenience site at the target Site post-Acquisition.
b. the Acquisition does not change the number of Ampol sites
or competitor sites in the area around the Target Site.
In these circumstances, the ACCC does not consider it necessary to
reach a concluded view on the likelihood of the notification
thresholds being met.
Given that material competition concerns are unlikely to arise, the
ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_declined.merger_description: Laundy Woy Freehold Pty Ltd and Laundy Woy Trading Pty Ltd (together, **Laundy)** propose to acquire the freehold land and operating business known as the Woy Woy Hotel, located at 33–39 The Boulevarde, Woy Woy, NSW from Amal Trustees Pty Limited as trustee for Harvest Pub Fund Trust 2 and Harvest Pub Operations 2 Limited, pursuant to a contract dated 20 April 2026.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by a division of the Commission constituted by a direction issued pursuant to section 19 of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Laundy Woy Freehold Pty Ltd and Laundy Woy Trading Pty Ltd
(together, Laundy) applied for a notification waiver in respect of its
proposed acquisition of the freehold land and operating business
known as the Woy Woy Hotel, located at 33–39 The Boulevarde, Woy
Woy NSW, from Amal Trustees Pty Limited as trustee for Harvest
Pub Fund Trust 2 and Harvest Pub Operations 2 Limited, as
described in the transaction documents provided as part of the
application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(b) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) not to make the notification waiver
determination applied for.

The acquirer, Laundy, operates a portfolio of licensed hospitality
venues across New South Wales, including pubs and hotels located
in metropolitan and regional areas. Laundy currently owns and
operates the Bayview Hotel located at 2/16, The Boulevard, Woy
Woy, NSW.
The Woy Woy Hotel is an existing licensed hotel business
comprising gaming, food and beverage, bottle shop and hospitality
services. It is located at 33-39 The Boulevard, Woy Woy, NSW.
The Acquisition involves the transfer of ownership of both the
freehold land and the operating hotel business to Laundy.
Woy Woy is a coastal town in the Central Coast region of the New
South Wales state of Australia. Woy Woy has two pubs, the Woy Woy
Hotel and the Bayview Hotel, and the nearest pub outside of Woy
Woy is in Umina.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) has considered the
information provided with the notification waiver application and had
regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Act.
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers
that the Acquisition may raise competition issues. In particular:
a. there is significant horizontal overlap between Laundy
and the Woy Woy Hotel in the supply of on-premises
hospitality products and services (including liquor for
on-site consumption, dining and gaming) in the Woy Woy
area and the acquisition would result in Laundy owning
both pubs in Woy Woy.
b. there is uncertainty regarding the level of competitive
constraint likely to be exerted by alternative suppliers of
on-premises hospitality products and services (including
liquor for on-site consumption, dining and gaming) in
Woy Woy and the surrounding area including from the
Country Club and RSL Club in Woy Woy and the Ocean
Beach Hotel in Umina.
The ACCC considers that these potential competition issues warrant
consideration through the notification process, including further
information gathering and consultation with third parties.
Given the above, the ACCC does not consider it necessary to reach a
concluded view on the likelihood of the Acquisition meeting the
notification thresholds. The ACCC notes that Laundy has
acknowledged that the proposed acquisition may meet the
notification thresholds.
For these reasons, the ACCC has determined not to make the
notification waiver determination applied for.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.
